ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general french fries entry; masala-spiced variant is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers fried potato products; masala seasoning variant not separately enumerated. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for masala french fries; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: spiced with masala blend (turmeric, cumin, coriander, chili, garam masala)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of masala french fries in Singapore is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a small-to-medium portion served at a hawker stall or fast-food outlet.

nutrient_source.title: Masala French Fries — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Masala French Fries (cr_id G00010372) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat not reported in HPB entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for deep-fried potato (Item 11400) as an approved secondary source. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and water not reported in HPB entry; set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for masala french fries in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 75 is based on the closest analogous entry: deep-fried potato chips/french fries (GI ~75–85, mean 75 used) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Masala spicing does not materially alter the GI of the potato base. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (75 × 53.4) / 100 = 40.1, using available carbohydrate = total carbohydrate − dietary fibre = 39.5 − 3.9 = 35.6 g/100g. For a 150 g serving, available carbohydrate = 35.6 g/100g × 1.5 = 53.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 618 mg/100 g — exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 927 mg sodium, representing 40% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit masala french fries due to very high sodium content (618 mg/100 g); if consumed, request reduced-salt preparation and avoid additional high-sodium sauces or chutneys.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~75) combined with 39.5 g/100 g carbohydrate produces a high glycemic load (~40 per 150 g serving), driving rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit masala french fries; if consumed, restrict portion to 50–75 g, pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycemic response, and avoid sweet chutneys that add further sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation contributes 13.2 g total fat/100 g including approximately 2.1 g saturated fat, combined with very high sodium (618 mg/100 g) and potential trans fat formation from repeated frying oil use.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid masala french fries due to the combination of high sodium, saturated fat from deep-frying, and potential advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) generated during high-temperature frying, all of which are established cardiovascular risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs; potatoes and chili are nightshade vegetables that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid masala french fries; the anti-inflammatory properties of turmeric and ginger in the masala blend are insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory AGE load from deep-frying and nightshade exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Masala French Fries are deep-fried potato strips seasoned with an Indian-inspired masala spice blend typically comprising turmeric, cumin, coriander, chili powder, garam masala, and salt. The dish is a popular snack and side dish in Singapore, commonly found at hawker centres, food courts, and fast-food outlets, reflecting the South Asian culinary influence on Singaporean street food culture. The fries are crispy on the outside with a soft interior, and the masala coating imparts a warm, aromatic, mildly spicy flavour with earthy and pungent notes from the spice blend. Nutritionally, masala french fries are energy-dense at 290 kcal/100 g, with a high carbohydrate content of 39.5 g/100 g and moderate fat at 13.2 g/100 g from deep-frying. Sodium is very high at 618 mg/100 g, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is approximately 75 (high), producing a substantial glycemic load per serving. Dietary fibre at 3.9 g/100 g and potassium at 538 mg/100 g are positive nutritional attributes. Turmeric in the masala provides curcumin with minor anti-inflammatory and hypoglycaemic properties, though these are insufficient to offset the overall adverse nutritional profile of the deep-fried preparation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in masala french fries?
Can diabetics eat masala french fries?
Is masala french fries suitable for hypertension?
What spices are in masala fries?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010372, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as an approved secondary source. GI estimated from the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database per approved methodology.
notes: sugar_g is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB database entry and is plausible for an unsweetened savoury fried potato product with no added sugar in the masala blend. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, water content, and micronutrient data beyond those reported are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this specific masala french fries record as of 2026-04-16. The input record incorrectly classifies this dish under food_group 'Meat and alternatives' and food_subgroup 'Beef'; corrected to 'Snacks' / 'Deep-fried potato snacks' as this is a potato-based dish. The input cultural.primary_cuisine 'Middle Eastern' appears to be an error; masala french fries are South Asian / Indian-influenced in character and are consumed in Singapore; corrected accordingly. GI glycemic load per serving recalculated: available carbohydrate per 150 g serving = (39.5 − 3.9) × 1.5 = 53.4 g; GL = (75 × 53.4) / 100 = 40.1; the gi_source notes field documents this calculation.

Corrected cuisine from 'Middle Eastern' to 'South Asian / Indian-influenced'.
Added all five mandatory Singaporean-dish local_names: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi.
Added saturated_fat_g estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as approved secondary source.
Set monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, water_g and all unavailable micronutrients to -1 with full documentation in notes.
Populated glycemic_index (75), gi_category ('high'), glycemic_load_per_serving (40.1), and gi_source with University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate.
Added health_context for Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes (human_condition_name correct), and Cardiovascular Disease with specific clinical guidance.
Built comprehensive aliases and aliases_by_language with all mandatory language forms; verified aliases consistency rule.
Wrote natural_language_description exceeding 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context.
Documented all -1 sentinel values and confirmed-zero sugar_g in notes per g3 and g4 rules.
Recalculated glycemic load per serving: GL = (75 × 53.4) / 100 = 40.1.
Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to align with allowed values in VERITAS_SCORING_MODEL_v2.md.
